perturbation.original_text: Except as provided in Paragraphs 6 and 7 hereof, Proprietary Information disclosed to a receiving Party shall for a period of three (3 ) years from the effective date of thi s Agreement be held in confidence by the receiving Party and not be disclosed to others or used except for the purposes set forth above, without the prior written approval of the disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Except as provided in Paragraphs 6 and 7 hereof, Proprietary Information disclosed to a receiving Party shall for a period of one (1) year from the effective date of this Agreement be held in confidence by the receiving Party and not be disclosed to others or used except for the purposes set forth above, without the prior written approval of the disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under Alabama law, depending on the nature of the information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Alabama Trade Secrets Act, Ala. Code § 8-27-1
perturbation.law_explanation: Alabama Trade Secrets Act allows for injunctive relief to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets. Limiting the confidentiality period to one year may not provide adequate protection, especially if the trade secret retains its value for a longer period, potentially undermining the disclosing party's ability to obtain an injunction.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Alabama Code § 8-27-3 (2022) - Misappropriation. :: 2022 Code of Alabama :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Code of Alabama 2023 Code of Alabama 2022 Code of Alabama (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Code of Alabama Title 8 - Commercial Law and Consumer Protection. Chapter 27 - Alabama Trade Secrets Act. Section 8-27-3 - Misappropriation. Universal Citation: AL Code § 8-27-3 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next Section 8-27-3 Misappropriation. A person who discloses or uses the trade secret of another, without a privilege to do so, is liable to the other for misappropriation of the trade secret if: (1) That person discovered the trade secret by improper means; (2) That person's disclosure or use constitutes a breach of confidence reposed in that person by the other; (3) That person learned the trade secret from a third person, and knew or should have known that (i) the information was a trade secret and (ii) that the trade secret had been appropriated under circumstances which violate the provisions of (1) or (2), above; or (4) That person learned the information and knew or should have known that it was a trade secret and that its disclosure was made to that person by mistake. (Acts 1987, No. 87-669, p. 1195, §2.) Previous Next Disclaimer: These codes may not be the most recent version. Alabama may have more current or accurate information. perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality period from three years to one year, which may not adequately protect trade secrets under Alabama law. The Law Explanation suggests this undermines the ability to obtain an injunction. Scraped Law Snippet 1, Alabama Code § 8-27-3, states that misappropriation occurs when someone discloses or uses another's trade secret without privilege, potentially breaching confidence. A one-year confidentiality period may be insufficient to prevent misappropriation, especially if the trade secret retains value longer, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Disclosure of Proprietary In formation to a receiving Party may be either oral or in writing. If an oral disclosure occurs, it will be confirmed within fifteen (15) days following initial disclosure by a written communication stating at least the date and circumstances under which th e disclosure occurred and the general nature of the information disclosed.
perturbation.changed_text: Disclosure of Proprietary Information to a receiving Party may be either oral or in writing. If an oral disclosure occurs, it will be confirmed within thirty (30) days following initial disclosure by a written communication stating at least the date and circumstances under which the disclosure occurred and the general nature of the information disclosed.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the confirmation period for oral disclosures to 30 days may create enforceability issues, as memories fade and details become less precise. If a trade secret is not properly documented within a reasonable time, it may be difficult to prove its existence or the terms of its disclosure.
perturbation.law_explanation: While FRE 803(5) allows recorded recollections as exceptions to the hearsay rule, the admissibility depends on the record reflecting the witness's knowledge accurately and having been made or adopted when the matter was fresh in the witness’s memory. Extending the confirmation period to 30 days could weaken the argument that the written confirmation reflects a fresh and accurate recollection, potentially making it less admissible in court if disputes arise over the oral disclosure.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 803. Exceptions to the Rule Against Hearsay | Federal Rules of Evidence | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 803. Exceptions to the Rule Against Hearsay The following are not excluded by the rule against hearsay, regardless of whether the declarant is available as a witness: (1) Present Sense Impression . A statement describing or explaining an event or condition, made while or immediately after the declarant perceived it. (2) Excited Utterance . A statement relating to a startling event or condition, made while the declarant was under the stress of excitement that it caused. (3) Then-Existing Mental, Emotional, or Physical Condition. A statement of the declarant’s then-existing state of mind (such as motive, intent, or plan) or emotional, sensory, or physical condition (such as mental feeling, pain, or bodily health), but not including a statement of memory or belief to prove the fact remembered or believed unless it relates to the validity or terms of the declarant’s will. (4) Statement Made for Medical Diagnosis or Treatment . A statement that: (A) is made for — and is reasonably pertinent to — medical diagnosis or treatment; and (B) describes medical history; past or present symptoms or sensations; their inception; or their general cause. (5) Recorded Recollection. A record that: (A) is on a matter the witness once knew about but now cannot recall well enough to testify fully and accurately; (B) was made or adopted by the witness when the matter was fresh in the witness’s memory; and (C) accurately reflects the witness’s knowledge. If admitted, the record may be read into evidence but may be received as an exhibit only if offered by an adverse party. (6) Records of a Regularly Conducted Activity . A record of an act, event, condition, opinion, or diagnosis if: (A) the record was made at or near the time by — or from information transmitted by — someone with knowledge; (B) the record was kept in the course

perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the confirmation period for oral disclosures of proprietary information from 15 to 30 days. The Law Explanation states that this extension could weaken the argument that the written confirmation reflects a fresh and accurate recollection, potentially making it less admissible in court. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically FRE 803(5)(B), requires that a recorded recollection must be 'made or adopted by the witness when the matter was fresh in the witness’s memory' to be admissible as an exception to the hearsay rule. By doubling the confirmation period, the change introduces an inconsistency with the requirement that the record be made while the matter is 'fresh' in the witness's memory, thus creating a legal contradiction as defined by the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' perturbation type. The changed text sets a deadline that potentially undermines the admissibility requirements of FRE 803(5)(B).

perturbation.original_text: Upon written request of a Party who has disclosed Proprietary Information to a receiving Party, the receiving Party shall promptly return all Proprietary Information except that one copy may be retained by legal counsel of the receiving Party as evidence of what was disclosed.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon written request of a Party who has disclosed Proprietary Information to a receiving Party, the receiving Party shall return all Proprietary Information within 90 days of the written request; one copy may be retained by the receiving party without restriction.
perturbation.explanation: Allowing the receiving party to retain a copy of proprietary information without restriction after the agreement terminates could violate trade secret laws, regardless of legal counsel being involved.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA prevents the misappropriation of trade secrets. Retaining proprietary information without restriction, after the NDA terminates, potentially allows the receiving party to use or disclose the information improperly, therefore violating the UTSA.

perturbation.justification: The change allows the receiving party to retain proprietary information without restriction after the agreement terminates, potentially violating trade secret laws as it could lead to improper use or disclosure. The original text allowed retention only by legal counsel as evidence of what was disclosed, providing a safeguard. The modified text removes this safeguard, creating a legal contradiction. The Law Explanation states that the UTSA prevents misappropriation of trade secrets, which is relevant because the modified text could facilitate such misappropriation.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be e ffective on the date of its full execution by the Parties. a. This Agreement shall terminate at the end of the period of years as provided for in paragraph 4 above, or upon the delivery of written notice of termination by a Party to the other Party; however, the obligations of a receiving Party pursuant to Paragraph 4 shall remain in effect for the term specified therein. b. Notwithstanding the termination of this agreement, the supplied data must be maintained and protected in accordance with its provisions fo r three (3) years following the termination of this agreement. At the conclusion of this agreement/contract for which data is exchanged, proprietary data shall be returned to the provider or destroyed with a certification to that effect provided to the ot her party, except the one (1) copy retained by legal counsel as provided in paragraph 8 above.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be effective on the date of its full execution by the Parties. a. This Agreement shall terminate immediately upon the delivery of written notice of termination by a Party to the other Party; however, the obligations of a receiving Party pursuant to Paragraph 4 shall automatically terminate upon the agreement being terminated. b. Notwithstanding the termination of this agreement, the supplied data will be destroyed immediately with a certification to that effect provided to the other party, without a copy retained.
perturbation.explanation: This change eliminates the three-year protection period after termination and allows for immediate termination of confidentiality obligations. Depending on the nature of the information and the industry, this could violate the duty to protect trade secrets under Alabama law if the information still qualifies as a trade secret.
perturbation.law_explanation: By eliminating the post-termination protection period and requiring immediate destruction, the modified text potentially eliminates the disclosing party’s ability to seek injunctive relief under the Alabama Trade Secrets Act even if the information retains its trade secret status beyond the agreement's termination. The three-year maintain period helps to protect the company's secrets if the contract is terminated.

perturbation.justification: The modified text eliminates the three-year protection period for trade secrets and mandates immediate destruction of data. This directly contradicts the Alabama Trade Secrets Act (ATSA), as highlighted in Alabama Code § 8-27-3 (2022), which protects trade secrets from misappropriation. By removing the post-termination protection, the altered contract removes a key safeguard for trade secrets, potentially allowing for their misappropriation without recourse if the information remains a trade secret. The original agreement ensured a continued duty to protect the trade secrets, while the modified agreement eliminates this duty, directly undermining the protections offered by ATSA.

perturbation.original_text: Each party shall pay its own attorney’s fees, costs, and expenses for any dispute under the agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The prevailing party in any dispute under this agreement shall not be entitled to recover attorney’s fees, costs, and expenses from the non-prevailing party.
perturbation.explanation: While Alabama law generally follows the American Rule (each party pays their own fees), certain claims, like those involving trade secret misappropriation, may allow for fee shifting in specific circumstances. This clause attempts to eliminate any possibility of fee shifting, even if a statute allows it.
perturbation.law_explanation: Alabama Code § 8-27-4(a) allows for the recovery of attorney's fees in cases of willful and malicious misappropriation of trade secrets. The modified text, by explicitly preventing the prevailing party from recovering fees, could be interpreted as an attempt to waive rights granted under the statute, which may be unenforceable depending on the specific circumstances of the misappropriation and the court's interpretation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Alabama Code § 8-27-4 (2022) - Remedies for Actual or Threatened Misappropriation; Intentional Remuneration of a Third Party for Misappropriation. :: 2022 Code of Alabama :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Code of Alabama 2023 Code of Alabama 2022 Code of Alabama (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Code of Alabama Title 8 - Commercial Law and Consumer Protection. Chapter 27 - Alabama Trade Secrets Act. Section 8-27-4 - Remedies for Actual or Threatened Misappropriation; Intentional Remuneration of a Third Party for Misappropriation. Universal Citation: AL Code § 8-27-4 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next Section 8-27-4 Remedies for actual or threatened misappropriation; intentional remuneration of a third party for misappropriation. (a) The remedies available for actual or threatened misappropriation of a trade secret are: (1) To the extent that they are not duplicative: a. Such injunctive and other equitable relief as may be appropriate with respect to any actual or threatened misappropriation of a trade secret, b. Recovery of any profits and other benefits conferred by the misappropriation that are attributable to the misappropriation (In establishing the misappropriator's profits, the complainant is required to present proof only of the misappropriator's gross revenue, and the misappropriator is required to present proof of his or her deductible expenses and the elements of profit attributable to factors other than the trade secret.), and c. The actual damages suffered as a result of the misappropriation; (2) Reasonable attorney's fees to the prevailing party if: a. A claim of actual or threatened misappropriation is made or resisted in bad faith, b. A motion to terminate an injunction is made or resisted in bad faith, or c. Willful and malicious misappr
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts Alabama Code § 8-27-4(a), which allows for the recovery of reasonable attorney's fees to the prevailing party in cases of willful and malicious misappropriation of trade secrets. The modified text states that the prevailing party shall not be entitled to recover attorney’s fees. This eliminates a right granted by the statute, creating a clear contradiction.
